metadata.venue: International Journal of Advanced Computer Science and Applications (IJACSA)

system_context.system_description: Compliance-driven smartphone based Electronic Health Record (EHR) system to meet customers need while ensuring compliance with controlling and legal requirements.
system_context.business_goals: Ensure and comply with applicable regulatory requirements (e.g., HIPAA, HITECH, ISO 9001:2015); strengthening patient rights, increasing efficiency, and decreasing administrative cost.

evaluation_setup.evaluation_goal: To evaluate compliance-driven EHR architecture vs. non-compliant EHR architecture (blackboard) and assess modifiability and compliance.

architectures.name: Blackboard EHR
architectures.description: Data-centered architecture style where components communicate through a shared database.
assumptions: Focus is on performance quality attribute.

architectures.name: LCA-based EHR
architectures.description: Client-server along with event-based implicit invocation style, restructured layered architecture style by providing an additional layer of Compliance.

key_components.responsibilities: Execute logics, prioritize rules, maintain log, apply HIPAA compliance rules.
Track compliance at software architecture level.

assumptions: Focus is on performance quality attribute and security compliance attribute.

scenarios.scenario_text: Controlled substances e-perception should be digitally signed before submission.
Interception of and tampering with communication.
Denial of service (DOS), sending large amount of data based on spoofed identifier.

scenario_classification.rationale: Classified as Indirect for Blackboard EHR (requires modification); Direct for LCA-based EHR.
Requires architectural change (SSL) for both architectures.
Requires architectural change (server monitoring) for both architectures.
Directly supported by LCA-based EHR; Direct for Blackboard EHR according to Table IV.
Directly supported by LCA-based EHR; Direct for Blackboard EHR according to Table IV.
Directly supported by LCA-based EHR; Direct for Blackboard EHR according to Table IV.

affected_components: All components that call submit prescription
scenario_evaluations.change_description: All components that call submit prescription must be modified.
issues_identified: Severe software architecture level limitations to achieve HIPAA compliance.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Use Secure Socket Layer (SSL) transport layer security
Implement server monitoring for high traffic from a particular user.

scenario_interactions.modularity_implication: Risk: Unauthorized PHI disclosure vs Non-Risk: Authorized PHI disclosure.

architectural_strengths: LCA-based EHR has better ability to meet HIPAA compliance requirements.
LCA-based EHR ensures improved data quality.
LCA-based EHR provides better access log management.
architectural_weaknesses: Blackboard EHR has severe software architecture level limitations to achieve HIPAA compliance.
Blackboard EHR provides limited HIPAA compliance and doesn't maintain log.
LCA-based EHR impacts performance (Order execution time increases).
overall_results.modifiability_assessment: LCA-based EHR supports compliance modifiability better than Blackboard EHR.

architecture_comparison.rationale: Performed better in terms of time but provides limited HIPAA compliance and does not maintain log; severe limitations for compliance.
Works better in terms of improved data quality, compliance with standards (HIPAA), eliminated error-prone coding, and better access log management.

recommendations: Revise existing EHR procedures to bridge the compliance gap using ISO 9001:2015 process driven approach.
Implement Secure Socket Layer (SSL) transport layer security.
Implement server monitoring for high traffic from a particular user.

missing_information: Explicit list of all stakeholders involved in the workshop.
Full detailed text for all 6 scenarios (only 3 fully detailed in text).
inference_notes: Inferred scenario names for CS 4, 5, 6 from Table V and Table VI mappings.
Inferred system maturity as 'Development' based on the context of proposing and evaluating a new architecture.
